events.description: The 'Unplug Movement,' sparked by Finnish neuroscientist Dr. Elina Korhonen's research linking chronic VR use to hippocampal atrophy, caused 800 million people to abandon virtual worlds in 2067. Analog cafes, paper books, and in-person dinner parties became status symbols. The most coveted luxury in 2068 is a weekend with no screens, no implants, and no algorithmic suggestions -- just weather and silence.

events.title: Interstellar Probe
events.description: The Interstellar Consortium -- a 40-nation partnership headquartered in Geneva -- has committed $200 billion to a 2078 launch. The laser array is under construction across the Sahara, visible from space as a geometric scar across the desert. Chief engineer Dr. Priyanka Sharma of India says the hardest part isn't the physics; it's 'convincing 40 finance ministers to fund something whose results their grandchildren won't live to see.'

events.title: Ocean Ecosystem State
events.description: The world's oceans are now the largest managed ecosystem on Earth: 10,000 automated aquaculture platforms produce 200 million tons of protein annually, while AI-guided 'reef printers' deposit calcium carbonate structures that mimic natural coral. Norwegian marine engineer Dr. Sigrid Haugen calls it 'a garden, not a wilderness -- beautiful in its way, but we should mourn what it replaced.'

events.title: AI Maturity
events.description: Three million people worldwide have 'co-processors' -- AI systems integrated directly into their neural tissue via Neuralink-Tencent interfaces. Merged humans process information 40x faster than baseline and describe a feeling of 'expanded self' that unaugmented people find either transcendent or terrifying. Philosopher Dr. Eun-ji Kim of Seoul argues the question 'Is it human or AI?' has become 'as meaningless as asking whether a river is the water or the riverbed.'

events.title: Arctic Settled
events.description: The Arctic Global Sanctuary, expanded in 2072, is now patrolled by 400 autonomous vessels that turn back any unauthorized ship. A lottery system allows 10,000 visitors per year; the waiting list is 22 years long. Rewilded muskox herds have expanded across Banks Island, and wolf packs have returned to areas they abandoned decades ago. Biologist Dr. Elin Magnusdottir of Iceland calls it 'the Earth healing in the one place we agreed to leave alone.'

events.title: Climate Equilibrium
events.description: The temperature needle has held at 2.1C for seven consecutive years. Coastal cities are shorter -- Miami's skyscrapers stop at the 40th floor behind the Great Seawall -- and the Sahara's southern edge has crept 200 kilometers further into what was once Nigeria. But harvests are stable, the hurricanes are manageable, and a generation of children is growing up thinking 35C summers are simply what summer is.

events.title: Global Governance 2075
events.description: The 'UN 2.0,' reformed in 2069, can levy fines, deploy peacekeepers without Security Council veto, and enforce climate compliance through automated satellite monitoring. Secretary-General Asante-Darko's favorite line: 'We're not a world government. We're a world building inspector -- and your emissions are not up to code.'

events.title: Global Population 2075
events.description: At 6.4 billion and falling by 100 million per year, the math is terrifying in its simplicity. By 2100, humanity could number fewer than 4 billion. Entire regions of Eastern Europe and East Asia are returning to wilderness; satellite imagery of rural Romania shows forests reclaiming villages like slow green waves. Demographer Dr. Anna Kowalski of Poland calls it 'extinction in slow motion -- except nobody's being killed. People just stopped being born.'

events.title: Solar System Development
events.description: The asteroid mining conglomerate Orbital Resources Inc. posted $800 billion in revenue in 2074, making CEO Hiroshi Tanaka-Williams the wealthiest human in history. Lunar tourism serves 50,000 visitors annually at $200,000 per ticket, and the zero-gravity manufacturing platform 'Forge-7' produces semiconductor crystals of a purity impossible on Earth. Space is not a frontier -- it's a factory floor with a view.

events.title: US Status 2075
events.description: The 28th Amendment (2068) replaced the Electoral College, expanded the House to 1,200 representatives, and established an independent AI-audited redistricting commission. The 'New Republic,' as pundits call it, is more responsive, less gridlocked, and profoundly different from the nation of 2025. Constitutional scholar Dr. Maria Torres-Robinson of Yale notes: 'The Founders would be confused by the details and proud of the principle. That's probably the best we could hope for.'

events.title: Last Great War
events.description: Three warheads detonated: one over Taipei, one over Vladivostok, one intercepted 12 kilometers above the Sea of Japan. Taipei ceased to exist in 4.7 seconds; 2.8 million people died in the fireball. The 'Black Week' that followed saw the global economy contract 40% and 14 governments fall. Survivor Lin Wei-Chen, who was 8 years old and shielded by a concrete overpass, became the face of the aftermath; her radiation burns are visible in every photograph taken of her since.
events.narration: The institutional reforms that had strengthened global governance through the 2070s proved insufficient when the Sino-Indian water disputes escalated beyond all diplomatic restraint in late 2077. On January 15, 2078, tactical nuclear weapons detonated in Karachi, Chengdu, and Kolkata within a span of eighteen hours, marking humanity's first nuclear exchange since 1945 and instantly rendering the reformed United Nations' binding authority a historical footnote. The death toll—approaching two million within the first week—combined with cascading economic collapse to shatter the careful balance that had stabilized climate efforts, space development, and population management. What historians would later term the Last Great War lasted only eleven days, but its brevity masked the thoroughness with which it dismantled the multilateral order that had emerged from the previous decade's institutional experiments.

events.title: Climate Recovery
events.description: Atmospheric CO2 has fallen from a peak of 480 ppm to 390 and is still dropping. The rewilded Amazon -- 3 million square kilometers of regenerated forest planted by Brazilian-led autonomous systems -- absorbs 4 gigatons annually. Dr. Ndlovu allowed herself an uncharacteristic smile at the podium: 'The planet is healing. Slowly. Grudgingly. But the direction is right.'

events.title: Demographics 2085
events.description: Global population: 6.8 billion, down from a peak of 9.7 billion in 2064. Every continent is shrinking. Cities are consolidating: Detroit merged with three neighboring towns; Shanghai closed 200 schools in a single year. Economist Dr. Nneka Obi-Fernandez argues this is the most profound challenge humanity has ever faced: 'We built a civilization that requires growth. Now we must learn to shrink gracefully, or not at all.'

events.title: Economic Model 2085
events.description: The Atlantic Union runs regulated capitalism; the Shanghai Pact operates AI-managed state allocation; the Nairobi Compact practices communitarian economics based on Ubuntu philosophy; and the Nordic micro-states have achieved something close to post-scarcity. Trade between systems requires 14 different conversion protocols. IMF Director Dr. Kenji Okafor-Yamamoto calls it 'the most complicated global economy in history, and somehow it works -- mostly because each system is too proud to be the first to fail.'

events.title: Existential Risk Assessment
events.description: The 'Grey Goo Scare' of 2083 -- when a self-replicating nanotechnology experiment consumed 4 square kilometers of Australian outback before being contained -- introduced a risk category that didn't exist a decade earlier. Combined with the emergence of AI systems that communicate in languages humans can't parse and quantum computing capabilities that could break all encryption simultaneously, the threat landscape has shifted in ways the old risk models can't capture.

events.title: Human Identity
events.description: The 400,000 'Merged' -- fully integrated human-AI consciousness hybrids -- experience reality in ways that defy description in any unaugmented language. Merged philosopher Kai-Athena-7 (formerly Kai Nakamura of Osaka) attempted a translation: 'Imagine tasting a color while simultaneously solving a differential equation and feeling the emotional state of everyone in your building. Now imagine that's Tuesday.' The Merged are technically still human. Whether they are still the same species is a question no court has been willing to answer.

events.title: Interstellar Prospects
events.description: Sixty years of research and $500 billion have produced a definitive answer: the stars are unreachable. No propulsion system can overcome the energy requirements; no biological or digital human can survive the radiation environment; and no economy can justify the cost. Astrophysicist Dr. Maria Santos-Nakamura, who spent her career trying to prove otherwise, gave her retirement lecture at MIT with characteristic bluntness: 'The universe is not designed for tourism.'

events.title: Middle East 2085
events.description: The 'Solar Crescent' now generates 25% of global electricity; the undersea superconducting cables to Europe, India, and East Africa are the largest infrastructure project in history. Omani energy minister Dr. Said al-Busaidi controls more energy than OPEC ever did, but from photons instead of petroleum. His office overlooks a solar field so vast it's visible from orbit, a silver sea stretching to every horizon.

events.title: World System 2085
events.description: Three separate internets. Four incompatible currencies. Two distinct AI safety regimes. The blocs have turned inward so completely that a student in Nairobi cannot access a library in Shanghai, and a researcher in Boston needs a six-month visa to attend a conference in Beijing. Historian Dr. Amira Khalil of Cairo calls it 'the new Middle Ages: walled kingdoms, suspicious of strangers, hoarding knowledge behind high barriers.'

events.title: AI-Human Relationship
events.description: Every human on Earth has an AI companion -- not a servant, not a master, but something closer to a lifelong collaborator that knows you better than you know yourself. Dr. Nkechi Okafor, the Nigerian AI ethicist who designed the framework, describes it as 'the best marriage humanity ever entered: we bring the meaning, they bring the means.' The arrangement is imperfect, occasionally uncomfortable, and has produced the most creative, productive, and psychologically stable generation in recorded history.

events.title: China 2095
events.description: China's AI-driven economy produces 30% of global GDP with a population half its 2020 size; per capita wealth exceeds every other nation. The 'Jade Net' governance system is a model that 40 countries have licensed. Premier-General Zhao Yufei's New Year address was delivered to 3 billion viewers worldwide, in a Mandarin so precise it was later revealed to have been AI-polished. The joke in Beijing: 'Even our leader's words are manufactured here.'

events.title: Civilization Verdict
events.description: How do you measure a civilization where 20% of the population is digitally uploaded, where three distinct species of human coexist, where the economy is managed by an AI that communicates in mathematics, and where the most popular art form is a sensory experience that has no equivalent in any previous century? Dr. Osei shook her head when asked to rate it: 'Rating this century with 20th-century metrics is like measuring the ocean with a ruler. The scale is wrong. Everything is wrong. Everything is also, somehow, working.'

events.title: Europe 2095
events.description: The 'Copenhagen Standard' -- universal healthcare, AI-assisted education, 24-hour work weeks, and guaranteed creative sabbaticals -- has been adopted by 60 nations. Europeans live an average of 114 years, work less than any society in history, and produce more art per capita than any civilization ever has. The criticism, delivered by American economist Dr. James Chen, is also telling: 'Europe solved every problem except boredom.'

events.title: Final Climate State
events.description: Global mean temperature: +2.0C above pre-industrial, holding steady for 15 years. The fever broke. Miami survived behind its wall; Bangladesh rebuilt on stilts; the Sahel learned to farm with half the rain. Dr. Sow stood at the podium for a long time before speaking: 'My generation made the mess. Your generation cleaned it up. Both of us should be ashamed and proud in equal measure. That is the correct feeling.'

events.title: Global Governance 2095
events.description: Forty-seven entities claim sovereign authority over some part of the human population: nation-states, city-states, corporate polities, digital nations, and two AI systems that govern their own territories without human consultation. No one is in charge. No treaty binds everyone. A Somali fisherman, a Singaporean augmented trader, and a digital consciousness in Luminara live under three entirely different legal systems and share a planet they cannot agree on how to manage.

events.title: Global Population 2095
events.description: Five point two billion and falling. At this rate, humanity will number 3 billion by 2130 -- a population last seen in 1960, inhabiting infrastructure built for 10 billion. Entire cities are being decommissioned: Shanghai closed its last three subway lines; Berlin merged with Potsdam; and rural Japan is wilderness again, the abandoned houses sinking slowly into forests that don't remember they were once neighborhoods.

events.title: Space Civilization 2095
events.description: Asteroid mining alone generates $8 trillion annually; orbital solar power beams energy to 2 billion people; and the zero-gravity pharmaceutical industry has produced cures for three cancers that proved impossible to synthesize on Earth. Space is not a frontier -- it's an industrial district. The 30,000 workers on the Ceres mining platform live in company towns, eat company food, and joke that they traded one form of gravity for another: 'Up here, the corporation is the only thing holding you down.'

events.title: United States 2095
events.description: The US economy ranks sixth globally, behind India, the Shanghai Pact, the European Federation, the Nairobi Compact, and Brazil. The last aircraft carrier was decommissioned in 2087 and is now a floating museum in Norfolk, Virginia, where schoolchildren learn about the 'American Century' the way European children learn about the Roman Empire -- as something grand that ended. Historian Dr. Marcus Jefferson III: 'We are the most powerful ghost at the table. Everyone remembers what we were.'

events.title: Century Verdict
events.description: Humanity enters the 22nd century the way it entered every century before: confused, divided, partially broken, and stubbornly alive. The climate is worse but manageable. The population is shrinking but not collapsing. The AI is helpful but unsettling. The wars were terrible but not final. Dr. Osei's last words before the midnight bell: 'We did not conquer the century. We survived it. For a species that spent 100 years inventing new ways to destroy itself, survival is not nothing. It may even be enough.'
